retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Porphyrin synthesis is the process that produces heme. Heme synthesis occurs partly in the mitochondria and partly in the cytosol. The biosynthesis involves an eight-step enzymatic pathway. Heme biosynthesis starts in mitochondria with the condensation of succinyl Co-A from the citric acid cycle and an amino acid glycine. They combine to produce a key heme intermediate, 5'-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) in mitochondria catalyzed by the pyridoxal phosphate-requiring (vitamin B6) enzyme, aminolevulinic acid synthase (ALAS). [3] This reaction is the rate-limiting step in the pathway. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: There are multiple steps involved in heme synthesis. Eight enzymes accomplish this process, four working in the mitochondria and four in the cytosol. The process starts in the mitochondria, where  ALA (aminolevulinic acid) synthase links glycine and succinyl coenzyme A to form ALA. Steps 2 through 5 occur in the cytosol. Next, ALA dehydratase takes two molecules of ALA and produces porphobilinogen (PBG). In the third step, porphobilinogen deaminase takes four molecules of PBG and produces hydroxymethylbilane. Next, uroporphyrinogen III cosynthase takes hydroxymethylbilane and produces uroporphyrinogen III. In the fifth step, uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase takes uroporphyrinogen III and produces coproporphyrinogen III. The final three steps of heme synthesis occur in the mitochondria. Coproporphyrinogen III is then transformed to protoporphyrinogen IX by coproporphyrinogen oxidase. The seventh step occurs when protoporphyrinogen oxidase converts protoporphyrinogen IX to protoporphyrin IX. The eighth and final step of heme synthesis is the addition of Fe to protoporphyrin IX by ferrochelatase, producing a heme molecule. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The two main components of hemoglobin synthesis are globin production and heme synthesis. Globin chain production occurs in the cytosol of erythrocyte precursors and occurs by genetic transcription and translation. Many studies have shown that the presence of heme induces globin gene transcription. Genes for the alpha chain are on chromosome 16, and genes for the beta chain are on chromosome 11. Heme synthesis occurs in both the cytosol and the mitochondria of erythrocytes precursors. It begins with glycine and succinyl coenzyme A and ends with the production of a protoporphyrin IX ring. The binding of the protoporphyrin to a Fe2+ ion forms the final heme molecule. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gastric Intrinsic Factor -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ado-Cbl is a cofactor for methylmalonyl CoA mutase enzyme. In this reaction, the methylmalonyl-CoA (a product from odd number fatty acid catabolism) converted to succinyl-CoA. The latter makes its way into two pathways: the citric acid cycle and heme synthesis. Accumulation of methylmalonyl-CoA responsible for: Neurological disorders due to the incorporation of methylmalonyl-CoA into the myelin sheath, and the result is neuron demyelination. Methylmalonic aciduria

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Function -- Cataplerotic Processes
retrieved_docs.content: Citric acid intermediates may leave the cycle to biosynthesize other compounds. Citrate can be diverted to fatty acid synthesis; alpha-ketoglutarate to amino acid synthesis, neurotransmitter synthesis, and purine synthesis; succinyl-CoA to heme synthesis; malate to gluconeogenesis and oxaloacetate to amino acid synthesis. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Function -- Cataplerotic Processes
retrieved_docs.content: Citric acid intermediates can leave the cycle to participate in the biosynthesis of other compounds. Citrate can be directed toward fatty acids, succinyl-CoA to heme synthesis, alpha-ketoglutarate to amino acid synthesis, purine and neurotransmitter synthesis, oxaloacetate to amino acid synthesis, and malate to gluconeogenesis. [14] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Following its synthesis, coproporphyrinogen III gets transported into mitochondria. The coproporphyrinogen III then gets decarboxylated by coproporphyrinogen oxidase enzyme to form the colorless product protoporphyrinogen IX.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Porphyria -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Heme is a 64 kDa tetrameric protein present in hemoglobin, myoglobin, respiratory cytochromes, and cytochrome P450 enzymes. [1] The heme biosynthetic pathway involves the conversion of the substrates glycine and succinyl coenzyme A to heme. The pathway consists of 8 enzymatic steps, four enzymes are present in the cytosol, and four enzymes are present in the mitochondria.

retrieved_docs.content: The interprofessional care must use an integrated care pathway using preferably an electronic medical recording linked to an evidence-based medical avenue to planning and peruse of all arthro-skeletal activities. Of note is that the earlier the manifestations of SSADH deficiency are discovered, the better the outcome is with the potential avoidance of most SUDEP occurrences. [Level III]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Hemin decreases the synthesis of ALA synthase 1 in three ways: Hemin reduces the synthesis of ALAS1 mRNA, destabilizes ALAS1 mRNA, and inhibits import of the enzyme ALAS1 from the cytosol into mitochondria.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: During the biosynthetic pathway, the linear hydroxymethylbilane can spontaneously form a “faulty” porphyrin ring when not immediately used as a substrate for uroporphyrinogen synthesis. If uroporphyrinogen III synthase is deficient, then hydroxymethylbilane spontaneously closes and forms a different molecule called uroporphyrinogen I. Uroporphyrinogen leads to the formation of coproporphyrinogen I. This molecule does not result in the formation of heme.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Heme is ferrous protoporphyrin IX- four pyrrole rings linked via methenyl bridges. The side chains of heme b are methyl, vinyl, methyl, propionyl, and asymmetric ring D: propionyl, methyl.

retrieved_docs.title: Succinic Semialdehyde Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the etiology of succinic semialdehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Review the typical clinical presentations of succinic semialdehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Explain the treatment and management options available for succinic semialdehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Summarize the importance of collaboration and coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance care coordination for patients affected with succinic semialdehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Function -- Cleavage of Succinyl Coenzyme A
retrieved_docs.content: The enzyme succinate thiokinase catalyzes the reversible interconversion of succinyl-CoA to succinate by cleaving succinyl CoA’s thioester bond. The enzyme uses inorganic phosphate and dinucleotide to produce trinucleotide and CoA. This coupled reaction is called substrate-level phosphorylation, like in glycolysis. [10]

Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1934
retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid metabolism disorder, indicating that individuals with nemoc may exhibit disturbances in amino‑acid metabolic pathways, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 356
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid uhelnatý, commonly known as carbon monoxide, is identified both as a gasotransmitery and as an antimetabolity. As a gasotransmitery, carbon monoxide functions as a small gaseous signaling molecule that influences vascular tone, neurotransmission, and anti‑inflammatory pathways in normal physiology. In its role as an antimetabolity, carbon monoxide interferes with cellular metabolic processes by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration, which can contribute to both therapeutic effects and toxic outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual classification as a gasotransmitery and an antimetabolity highlights its importance in understanding its physiological signaling functions, potential pharmacologic applications, and the management of exposure‑related toxicity in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2929
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymology, the scientific study of enzymes and their biochemical actions, is recognized as a relevant qualifier for several distinct medical topics. In the context of Cannabis, enzymology helps elucidate how the plant’s cannabinoids are metabolized, how enzyme‑mediated pathways influence its pharmacodynamics, and how enzymatic interactions may affect both therapeutic benefits and adverse effects. Enzymology also provides essential insight into فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), where enzyme‑dependent processes such as heme synthesis, iron transport, and erythropoiesis are central to the disorder’s pathophysiology and to the development of targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Additionally, enzymology is pertinent to gout and other crystal arthropathies, because enzyme‑driven metabolic pathways—particularly those governing purine turnover, uric acid production, and crystal formation—underlie the onset and progression of these conditions. By integrating enzymological knowledge across Cannabis use, iron‑deficiency anemia, and gout‑related crystal arthropathies, clinicians and researchers can achieve a more comprehensive understanding of disease mechanisms, improve diagnostic accuracy, and tailor interventions that consider the underlying enzymatic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. This categorization reflects their roles in metabolism and biochemistry, as both participate in key physiological pathways typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 314
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives comprise several biologically important compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine, each of which is classified as a member of this chemical group. These molecules play key roles in cellular metabolism, energy production, methyl‑group transfer, and nitrogen balance, making them essential for normal physiological function and for the management of various metabolic disorders. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency and related metabolic conditions, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives category; it is a protein‑based therapeutic rather than an amino‑acid‑derived compound. Recognizing the correct classification of these entities supports accurate understanding of their mechanisms of action and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide, referred to in Czech as oxid uhelnatý, is classified both as a gasotransmitter and as an antimetabolite. As a gasotransmitter, it functions as a small, endogenously produced gaseous signaling molecule that can modulate vascular tone, neurotransmission, and inflammatory responses. Its antimetabolite properties allow it to interfere with normal metabolic pathways, for example by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration. Exposure to carbon monoxide is directly associated with carbon‑monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a clinical syndrome characterized by hypoxic injury, neurological deficits, and potentially fatal outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual role as a physiological messenger and a toxic agent is essential for accurate diagnosis, prevention, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: provides multiple iron salts (ferrous fumarate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous cysteine glycinate) together with vitamin C derivatives (ascorbic acid, magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) that facilitate iron uptake, and a spectrum of B‑vitamins and cofactors that support erythropoiesis and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how specific oral iron preparations—whether bivalent iron‑ascorbate salts or broader multivitamin‑iron combinations—are organized within therapeutic classes and formulated to maximize iron bioavailability while addressing concurrent micronutrient deficiencies commonly seen in patients with iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is a pharmaceutical preparation that combines iron with folic acid and is classified as a member of the iron‑in‑combination‑with‑folic‑acid group, reflecting its role in providing both elemental iron and folate for the treatment of iron deficiency and support of folate-dependent metabolic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1409
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium citrate is a chemical compound that is derived from citric acid, with citric acid serving as its parent molecule.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 570
retrieved_docs.content: regulators of biosynthetic pathways. Recognizing these relationships clarifies how antimetabolite and immunosuppressive agents are organized, their mechanistic targets, biosynthetic requirements, and potential for resistance, providing essential insight for patient management, metabolic research, and therapeutic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.content: entities illustrate the diverse therapeutic roles of magnesium salts, iron compounds and fibrates, while emphasizing common safety considerations—particularly the avoidance of magnesium‑based agents and fibrates in patients with renal impairment and the avoidance of fibrates in hepatic steatosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1945
retrieved_docs.content: Phenyl salicylate is classified as a carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product, indicating that it belongs to the broader chemical class of substances that incorporate a carboxyl functional group or its derivatives. This relationship underscores the chemical nature of phenyl salicylate and situates it within the larger family of carboxylic acid‑related compounds, which is relevant for understanding its pharmacological properties, formulation considerations, and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2534
retrieved_docs.content: ANABOLIC AGENTS FOR SYSTEMIC USE are classified as synthetic androgens, which are man‑made compounds that mimic the biological activity of natural male sex hormones such as testosterone. As synthetic androgens, these agents are administered systemically to produce anabolic effects—including enhanced protein synthesis, muscle growth, and bone density—and are employed in therapeutic settings such as hormone‑deficient states, certain anemias, and muscle‑wasting disorders. Their synthetic nature also confers potential androgenic side effects, necessitating careful medical oversight during use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: phosphatidylserine, betaine, succinic acid). Iron‑containing multivitamins are classified under “IRON/MULTIVITAMINS” and “iron in other combinations,” and they employ diverse iron sources—including polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, ferric pyrophosphate, ferrous fumarate, ferro‑cysteine‑glycinate, and iron ascorbate—to enhance absorption, especially when paired with vitamin C (ascorbic acid 40 mg) and folic acid (1 mg). Antianemic preparations illustrate the link between micronutrient status and anemia; they combine iron with folic acid and vitamin B12 (or B‑complex) in products such as bivalent oral iron, iron‑folic‑acid tablets, and non‑iron antianemic agents. Laboratory monitoring of serum or whole‑blood vitamin B12 and folic acid guides repletion. Representative formulations include bariatric‑specific multivitamins with iron, prenatal multivitamins containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide,
